standards.desc: Recognize the distinguishing features of a sentence

currentEasyExamples.prompt: A sentence always starts with a:
A sentence always ends with a:
choices: Period, question mark, or exclamation point
currentEasyExamples.correct: Period, question mark, or exclamation point

standards.desc: Orally produce single-syllable words by blending sounds, including consonant blends

currentEasyExamples.prompt: Blend these sounds together to make a word: /s/ ... /u/ ... /n/. What word do you make?
Blend these sounds together to make a word: /f/ ... /l/ ... /a/ ... /g/. What word do you make?

standards.desc: Isolate and pronounce initial, medial vowel, and final sounds in spoken single-syllable words

currentEasyExamples.prompt: What is the FIRST sound in the word **map**?
What is the MIDDLE sound in the word **big**?

standards.desc: Segment spoken single-syllable words into their complete sequence of individual sounds

currentEasyExamples.prompt: How many sounds in the word **stop**?

standards.desc: Read grade-level text orally with accuracy, appropriate rate, and expression

currentEasyExamples.prompt: When you see an exclamation point (!), you should read with:
When you see a question mark (?), your voice should:

standards.desc: Use context to confirm or self-correct word recognition and understanding

currentEasyExamples.prompt: If you read a sentence and it doesn't make sense, a good reader will:
choices: Reread it and try to fix the mistake
currentEasyExamples.correct: Reread it and try to fix the mistake

standards.desc: Know the spelling-sound correspondences for common consonant digraphs

currentEasyExamples.prompt: Which word begins with the same sound you hear at the start of 'shoe'?
Which two letters make the "chh" sound like in **chip**?

standards.desc: Decode regularly spelled one-syllable words

currentEasyExamples.prompt: Which word names a small, furry pet that often says 'meow'?

standards.desc: Know final -e and common vowel team conventions for representing long vowel sounds

currentEasyExamples.prompt: Add an E to the end of **cap**. What word do you get?
What word do you make when you add a Magic E to the end of **hop**?

standards.desc: Use knowledge that every syllable must have a vowel sound to determine the number of syllables in a printed word

currentEasyExamples.prompt: How many syllables are in the word **cat**?
How many syllables are in the word **rabbit**?

standards.desc: Decode two-syllable words following basic patterns by breaking the words into syllables

currentEasyExamples.prompt: How do you break **napkin** into syllables?
How do you break **rabbit** into syllables?

standards.desc: Read words with inflectional endings

currentEasyExamples.prompt: What does the **-s** at the end of **cats** mean?
What is the root word in **playing**?

standards.desc: Recognize and read grade-appropriate irregularly spelled words

currentEasyExamples.prompt: Look at these words. Which one is a tricky word?
Which word says **have**?

standards.desc: Read on-level text with purpose and understanding

currentEasyExamples.prompt: What is the main reason the author wrote the story about Lily and her ball?
choices: To tell you about a sunny morning.
To teach you how to throw a ball.
To show how Lily solved a problem.
To help you learn about red balls.
currentEasyExamples.correct: To show how Lily solved a problem.

currentEasyExamples.prompt: Read the text about butterflies.Butterflies start as small eggs. The eggs hatch into tiny caterpillars. Caterpillars eat many leaves to grow bigger. T
choices: To make you laugh about caterpillars.
To teach you how butterflies grow.
To help you find butterflies outside.
To tell you a story about a flying butterfly.
currentEasyExamples.correct: To teach you how butterflies grow.

standards.desc: Know and apply grade-level phonics and word analysis skills in decoding words.

currentEasyExamples.prompt: Which word has a long vowel sound?
What is the root word in **playing**?

standards.desc: Distinguish long and short vowels when reading regularly spelled one-syllable words.

currentEasyExamples.prompt: Which word has a SHORT **a** sound?
Which word has a LONG **o** sound?

standards.desc: Know spelling-sound correspondences for additional common vowel teams.

currentEasyExamples.prompt: In the word **boat,** what sound does the vowel team **oa** make?
Which word has the **ee** vowel team?

standards.desc: Decode regularly spelled two-syllable words with long vowels.

currentEasyExamples.prompt: Break the word **cupcake** into two syllables:
Which two-syllable word has a long **i** sound?

standards.desc: Decode words with common prefixes and suffixes.

currentEasyExamples.prompt: The suffix **-ful** in **hopeful** means —
What does it mean if something is "helpful"?

standards.desc: Identify words with inconsistent but common spelling-sound correspondences.

currentEasyExamples.prompt: If you were trying to sound out each word below, which one would be the trickiest to read because its letter sounds don't follow the usual phonics rul
The word **have** is tricky because —
choices: It ends with 'e' but the 'a' is short
The 'a' doesn't make the expected sound
currentEasyExamples.correct: It ends with 'e' but the 'a' is short

standards.desc: Recognize and read grade-appropriate irregularly spelled words.

currentEasyExamples.prompt: Choose the correct spelling:
Choose the correct spelling of the word that means **one more time**:

standards.desc: Read with sufficient accuracy and fluency to support comprehension.

currentEasyExamples.prompt: Imagine you are reading a fun story to your classmates. Which way of reading would help everyone understand the story best?
choices: Reading very fast, almost like a race.
Reading each word slowly, stopping often to figure it out.
Reading smoothly, like you are telling the story, and saying most words correctly.
Reading so quietly that only you can hear yourself.
currentEasyExamples.correct: Reading smoothly, like you are telling the story, and saying most words correctly.

currentEasyExamples.prompt: When you see an exclamation mark (!) at the end of a sentence, you should read it with —

standards.desc: Read grade-level text with purpose and understanding.

currentEasyExamples.prompt: What did the farmer do to help his garden grow before summer?
choices: He watered the seeds and planted them.
He made soup with his vegetables.
He waited for the snow to fall.
currentEasyExamples.correct: He watered the seeds and planted them.

currentEasyExamples.prompt: Before you start reading, it helps to ask yourself —
choices: What do I want to learn from this?
currentEasyExamples.correct: What do I want to learn from this?

standards.desc: Read grade-level text orally with accuracy, appropriate rate, and expression.

currentEasyExamples.prompt: How should you read this sentence? "I can't believe we won the game!"
When you read a question aloud, your voice should —

standards.desc: Use context to confirm or self-correct word recognition and understanding, rereading as necessary.

currentEasyExamples.prompt: You read: "The dog buried a bone in the yard." But you accidentally read **bone** as "bun." What should you do?
choices: Go back and reread because 'bun' doesn't make sense
Ask someone to read for you
currentEasyExamples.correct: Go back and reread because 'bun' doesn't make sense

currentEasyExamples.prompt: What is a good strategy when a sentence doesn't make sense?
choices: Reread the sentence and try the hard word again
currentEasyExamples.correct: Reread the sentence and try the hard word again

standards.desc: Know and apply grade-level phonics and word analysis skills in decoding words.

currentEasyExamples.prompt: What is the root word in **uncomfortable**?
How many syllables are in the word **important**?

standards.desc: Identify and know the meaning of the most common prefixes and derivational suffixes.

currentEasyExamples.prompt: The prefix **dis-** in **disagree** means —
What does the suffix **-ment** mean in the word **enjoyment**?

standards.desc: Decode words with common Latin suffixes.

currentEasyExamples.prompt: What sound does **-tion** make at the end of a word?
The word "active" means "full of action." Which word below uses a common Latin suffix to show that something has a lot of a certain quality?

standards.desc: Read grade-appropriate irregularly spelled words.

currentEasyExamples.prompt: Which sentence uses the word "through" correctly?
choices: The children walked through the big gate.
I through the ball for the dog to catch.
The tiny bird flew through the deep ocean.
She wore a hat through her head.
currentEasyExamples.correct: The children walked through the big gate.

currentEasyExamples.prompt: Which of these words has a silent **w**?

standards.desc: Read with sufficient accuracy and fluency to support comprehension.

currentEasyExamples.prompt: Which choice best describes how a reader should say the sentence, "Wow, what a surprise!"
choices: In a quiet, calm voice, like telling a secret.
With a voice that shows excitement and wonder.
In a bored, flat voice, like reading a list.
With a slow, sad voice, like something is wrong.
currentEasyExamples.correct: With a voice that shows excitement and wonder.

currentEasyExamples.prompt: When a student reads a story fluently, what helps them understand the story best?
choices: Reading so fast that some words are skipped.
Stopping often to ask what every single word means.
Reading each word very slowly, one at a time.
Reading smoothly, making the words sound like talking.
currentEasyExamples.correct: Reading smoothly, making the words sound like talking.

standards.desc: Read grade-level text with purpose and understanding.

currentEasyExamples.prompt:
The octopus is a master of disguise. It can change its color, texture, and even shape to match its surroundings in less than a second.

What makes the
choices: It can change color, texture, and shape instantly
currentEasyExamples.correct: It can change color, texture, and shape instantly

currentEasyExamples.prompt: Setting a purpose for reading means —
choices: Knowing what you want to learn or find out before you start
currentEasyExamples.correct: Knowing what you want to learn or find out before you start

standards.desc: Follow words from left to right, top to bottom, and page by page

currentEasyExamples.prompt: After you have read all the words on one page of your book, what is the best way to find the next part of the story?
choices: Close the book and pick a new one.
Turn to the next page to continue reading.
Read the same page again from the beginning.
Look for words in the middle of the page you just finished.
currentEasyExamples.correct: Turn to the next page to continue reading.

currentEasyExamples.prompt: When you finish reading a LINE of words, where do you look next?
choices: Down to the next line on the left side
To the right of the line
currentEasyExamples.correct: Down to the next line on the left side

standards.desc: Recognize that spoken words are represented in written language by specific sequences of letters

currentEasyExamples.prompt: Words are made up of...
If you change the letters in a word, what happens?
choices: You get a different word or a nonsense word
currentEasyExamples.correct: You get a different word or a nonsense word

standards.desc: Understand that words are separated by spaces in print

currentEasyExamples.prompt:
In print, we put a space between words. How many words are in this line?

big dog
What do we put between words so we can tell them apart in print?

standards.desc: Recognize and name all upper- and lowercase letters of the alphabet

currentEasyExamples.prompt: Which is the UPPERCASE (capital) version of the letter "a"?
Which is the LOWERCASE version of the letter "G"?

standards.desc: Recognize and produce rhyming words

currentEasyExamples.prompt: Which pair of words rhymes?

standards.desc: Count, pronounce, blend, and segment syllables in spoken words

currentEasyExamples.prompt: How many SYLLABLES (claps) are in the word "cat"?
How many SYLLABLES (claps) are in the word "banana"?

standards.desc: Blend and segment onsets and rimes of single-syllable spoken words

currentEasyExamples.prompt: Blend these sounds together to make a word: /c/ ... 'at'. What word do you hear?
Put the onset and rime together: /s/ ... 'un'. What word do they make?

standards.desc: Isolate and pronounce the initial, medial vowel, and final sounds (phonemes) in three-phoneme (CVC) words

currentEasyExamples.prompt: Which word has the /i/ sound in the middle?
What is the MIDDLE sound in the word "pig"?

standards.desc: Add or substitute individual sounds (phonemes) in simple, one-syllable words to make new words

currentEasyExamples.prompt: Let's play with sounds! Say the word 'map'. Now, change the very first sound, /m/, to the /t/ sound. What new word do you hear?
Look at these pairs of words. In which pair was a new word made by changing only one sound?

standards.desc: Demonstrate basic knowledge of one-to-one letter-sound correspondences by producing the primary sound for each consonant

currentEasyExamples.prompt: Which word starts with the /m/ sound, like in "man"?
The letter B makes the /b/ sound. Which word starts with the /b/ sound?

standards.desc: Associate the long and short sounds with the common spellings (graphemes) for the five major vowels

currentEasyExamples.prompt: The word "cake" has a long 'a' sound, and "cat" has a short 'a' sound. Now, look at the word "rake". What kind of 'a' sound do you hear in "rake"?
Which word has a SHORT **i** sound (like in "pig")?

standards.desc: Read common high-frequency words by sight (e.g., the, of, to, you, she, my, is, are, do, does)

currentEasyExamples.prompt: Read this sentence: 'I see ___ sun.' Which word fits in the blank?
Finish the sentence: 'I want ___ run.' Which word fits in the blank?

standards.desc: Distinguish between similarly spelled words by identifying the sounds of the letters that differ

currentEasyExamples.prompt: Listen to the words MAP and MOP. Which sound changes in the middle of these words?
If you change the **D** in "dog" to **L**, what new word do you get?

standards.desc: Read emergent-reader texts with purpose and understanding

currentEasyExamples.prompt:
I see a cat. The cat is big. The cat is on the mat.

Where is the CAT?

I like dogs. I like cats. I like fish. I like all pets!

What is this STORY about?
